title: Setting up a rooting and enrichment area for pigs
subtitle: Pigs need to root. Giving them a place to do it prevents damage everywhere else.
excerpt: Pigs are highly intelligent and highly motivated to root and investigate their environment. A pig confined to a bare paddock with nothing to engage with will use its snout on the fencing, the housing, and anything else within reach. A well-designed rooting area gives pigs an appropriate outlet and measurably reduces destructive behaviour.
sourceNotes: DEFRA Code of Practice for the Welfare of Pigs, 2003. AHDB Pork, Enrichment for Outdoor Pigs factsheet. Compassion in World Farming, Pigs enrichment technical note.

glossaryTerms.definition: The natural behaviour of pigs in which they use their snout to push into and turn over soil, leaf litter, or other substrate while searching for food. Highly motivated: a pig deprived of rooting substrate will redirect the behaviour to whatever is available.

Materials or structures provided to pigs to occupy their natural investigative and rooting drive. Under the Welfare of Farmed Animals Regulations, pigs must have permanent access to enrichment material. Simple options include hanging hessian, straw bales, logs, and rooting piles.

content.text: UK law requires pigs to have constant access to
materials. On an outdoor system, the paddock provides some
opportunity, but a dedicated rooting area with fresh material works far better than worn-out ground.

For a pair of weaners, a rooting pile of fresh compost, wood chip, or straw changed weekly is enough. Pile it 30-40 cm deep in one corner of the paddock and allow the pigs to work through it. Refresh the pile when the pigs have flattened it. Rotating the pile position every few weeks also prevents one area of the paddock becoming deeply poached.

A more permanent solution is a rooting bay: a 3 m x 3 m area fenced off within or adjacent to the main paddock, filled with topsoil, wood chip, leaf litter, or a mix of all three. The pigs have access to it but it is separate from the sleeping and feeding areas, keeping those areas cleaner. Refresh the rooting bay with a fresh load of material every two to four weeks.

Hanging a rope, a chain, or a large piece of hessian from the arc roof gives the pigs something to investigate and manipulate. A large log rolled into the paddock occupies a pair of pigs for hours. Scatter grain into fresh straw rather than feeding from a trough occasionally; the foraging effort occupies time and reduces tail-biting and pen vices.

Wood chip from an untreated hardwood source is the best rooting substrate for a smallholding: cheap, often free from tree surgeons, long-lasting, and good for drainage. Avoid chip from conifers (resinous and acidic) and any treated timber.
